lines.speaker: CICERO
lines.text: Good even, Casca: brought you Caesar home?

lines.speaker: CICERO
lines.text: Why are you breathless? and why stare you so?

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: Are not you moved, when all the sway of earth

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: Shakes like a thing unfirm? O Cicero,

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: I have seen tempests, when the scolding winds

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: Have rived the knotty oaks, and I have seen

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: The ambitious ocean swell and rage and foam,

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: To be exalted with the threatening clouds:

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: But never till to-night, never till now,

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: Did I go through a tempest dropping fire.

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: Either there is a civil strife in heaven,

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: Or else the world, too saucy with the gods,

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: Incenses them to send destruction.

lines.speaker: CICERO
lines.text: Why, saw you anything more wonderful?

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: A common slave—you know him well by sight—

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: Held up his left hand, which did flame and burn

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: Like twenty torches join’d, and yet his hand,

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: Not sensible of fire, remain’d unscorch’d.

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: Besides—I ha’ not since put up my sword—

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: Against the Capitol I met a lion,

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: Who glared upon me, and went surly by,

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: Without annoying me: and there were drawn

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: Upon a heap a hundred ghastly women,

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: Transformed with their fear; who swore they saw

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: Men all in fire walk up and down the streets.

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: And yesterday the bird of night did sit

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: Even at noon-day upon the market-place,

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: Hooting and shrieking. When these prodigies

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: Do so conjointly meet, let not men say

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: “These are their reasons; they are natural;”

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: For, I believe, they are portentous things

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: Unto the climate that they point upon.

lines.speaker: CICERO
lines.text: Indeed, it is a strange-disposed time:

lines.speaker: CICERO
lines.text: But men may construe things after their fashion,

lines.speaker: CICERO
lines.text: Clean from the purpose of the things themselves.

lines.speaker: CICERO
lines.text: Come Caesar to the Capitol to-morrow?

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: He doth; for he did bid Antonius

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: Send word to you he would be there to-morrow.

lines.speaker: CICERO
lines.text: Good night then, Casca: this disturbed sky

lines.speaker: CICERO
lines.text: Is not to walk in.

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: Farewell, Cicero.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Who’s there?

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: A Roman.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Casca, by your voice.

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: Your ear is good. Cassius, what night is this!

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: A very pleasing night to honest men.

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: Who ever knew the heavens menace so?

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Those that have known the earth so full of faults.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: For my part, I have walk’d about the streets,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Submitting me unto the perilous night,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: And, thus unbraced, Casca, as you see,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Have bared my bosom to the thunder-stone;

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: And when the cross blue lightning seem’d to open

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: The breast of heaven, I did present myself

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Even in the aim and very flash of it.

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: But wherefore did you so much tempt the heavens?

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: It is the part of men to fear and tremble,

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: When the most mighty gods by tokens send

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: Such dreadful heralds to astonish us.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: You are dull, Casca, and those sparks of life

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: That should be in a Roman you do want,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Or else you use not. You look pale and gaze

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: And put on fear and cast yourself in wonder,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: To see the strange impatience of the heavens:

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: But if you would consider the true cause

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Why all these fires, why all these gliding ghosts,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Why birds and beasts from quality and kind,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Why old men fool and children calculate,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Why all these things change from their ordinance

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Their natures and preformed faculties

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: To monstrous quality—why, you shall find

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: That heaven hath infused them with these spirits,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: To make them instruments of fear and warning

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Unto some monstrous state.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Now could I, Casca, name to thee a man

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Most like this dreadful night,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: That thunders, lightens, opens graves, and roars

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: As doth the lion in the Capitol,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: A man no mightier than thyself or me

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: In personal action, yet prodigious grown

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: And fearful, as these strange eruptions are.

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: ’Tis Caesar that you mean; is it not, Cassius?

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Let it be who it is: for Romans now

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Have thews and limbs like to their ancestors;

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: But, woe the while! our fathers’ minds are dead,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: And we are govern’d with our mothers’ spirits;

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Our yoke and sufferance show us womanish.

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: Indeed, they say the senators to-morrow

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: Mean to establish Caesar as a king;

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: And he shall wear his crown by sea and land,

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: In every place, save here in Italy.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: I know where I will wear this dagger then;

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Cassius from bondage will deliver Cassius:

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Therein, ye gods, you make the weak most strong;

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Therein, ye gods, you tyrants do defeat:

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Nor stony tower, nor walls of beaten brass,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Nor airless dungeon, nor strong links of iron,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Can be retentive to the strength of spirit;

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: But life, being weary of these worldly bars,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Never lacks power to dismiss itself.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: If I know this, know all the world besides,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: That part of tyranny that I do bear

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: I can shake off at pleasure.

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: So can I:

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: So every bondman in his own hand bears

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: The power to cancel his captivity.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: And why should Caesar be a tyrant then?

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Poor man! I know he would not be a wolf,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: But that he sees the Romans are but sheep:

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: He were no lion, were not Romans hinds.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Those that with haste will make a mighty fire

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Begin it with weak straws: what trash is Rome,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: What rubbish and what offal, when it serves

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: For the base matter to illuminate

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: So vile a thing as Caesar! But, O grief,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Where hast thou led me? I perhaps speak this

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Before a willing bondman; then I know

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: My answer must be made. But I am arm’d,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: And dangers are to me indifferent.

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: You speak to Casca, and to such a man

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: That is no fleering tell-tale. Hold, my hand:

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: Be factious for redress of all these griefs,

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: And I will set this foot of mine as far

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: As who goes farthest.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: There’s a bargain made.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Now know you, Casca, I have moved already

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Some certain of the noblest-minded Romans

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: To undergo with me an enterprise

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Of honourable-dangerous consequence;

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: And I do know, by this, they stay for me

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: In Pompey’s porch: for now, this fearful night,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: There is no stir or walking in the streets;

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: And the complexion of the element

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: In favour’s like the work we have in hand,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Most bloody, fiery, and most terrible.

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: Stand close awhile, for here comes one in haste.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: ’Tis Cinna; I do know him by his gait;

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: He is a friend.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Cinna, where haste you so?

lines.speaker: CINNA
lines.text: To find out you. Who’s that? Metellus Cimber?

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: No, it is Casca; one incorporate

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: To our attempts. Am I not stay’d for, Cinna?

lines.speaker: CINNA
lines.text: I am glad on’t. What a fearful night is this!

lines.speaker: CINNA
lines.text: There’s two or three of us have seen strange sights.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Am I not stay’d for? tell me.

lines.speaker: CINNA
lines.text: Yes, you are.

lines.speaker: CINNA
lines.text: O Cassius, if you could

lines.speaker: CINNA
lines.text: But win the noble Brutus to our party—

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Be you content: good Cinna, take this paper,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: And look you lay it in the praetor’s chair,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Where Brutus may but find it; and throw this

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: In at his window; set this up with wax

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Upon old Brutus’ statue: all this done,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Repair to Pompey’s porch, where you shall find us.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Is Decius Brutus and Trebonius there?

lines.speaker: CINNA
lines.text: All but Metellus Cimber; and he’s gone

lines.speaker: CINNA
lines.text: To seek you at your house. Well, I will hie,

lines.speaker: CINNA
lines.text: And so bestow these papers as you bade me.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: That done, repair to Pompey’s theatre.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Come, Casca, you and I will yet ere day

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: See Brutus at his house: three parts of him

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Is ours already, and the man entire

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Upon the next encounter yields him ours.

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: O, he sits high in all the people’s hearts:

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: And that which would appear offence in us,

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: His countenance, like richest alchemy,

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: Will change to virtue and to worthiness.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Him and his worth and our great need of him

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: You have right well conceited. Let us go,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: For it is after midnight; and ere day

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: We will awake him and be sure of him.

stage_directions.text: Thunder and lightning. Enter from opposite sides, Casca, with his sword drawn, and Cicero.

glosses.definition: the stable order of the earth; its governance
split; cloven
the grasping, rising sea — 'ambition' as a moral fault even in nature
a storm with lightning — the sky seen as raining fire
impudent toward the gods
not feeling the fire; insensible to heat
sheathed my drawn sword
women pale as ghosts; terrified
owl — an ill-omened bird; an owl's appearance at noon was a Roman portent
supernatural portents; omens of events to come — Roman religion placed great weight on reading them
things serving as portents; ominous signs
interpret signs in ways that suit them — a skeptical Ciceronian note against superstition
Gaius Cassius Longinus, Caesar's brother-in-law (married to Brutus's half-sister Junia); ringleader of the conspiracy
with doublet unlaced and open — breast bared to the storm
thunderbolt; lightning cast as a stone from heaven
forked blue lightning

glosses.phrase: It is the part of men
glosses.definition: it is the role/duty of men
messengers (of the gods) sent to stun us
throw yourself into amazement; give yourself over to awe
children speak prophecy (normally reserved for augurs and old men)
natural abilities assigned at creation
means by which the gods alert mortals to coming danger
monstrous; portentous — having the quality of a prodigy
muscles and body parts; physical strength
ruled by softer, feminine temperaments rather than warrior fathers'
submission and patient endurance — the condition of a slave

glosses.phrase: I know where I will wear this dagger
glosses.definition: Cassius threatens suicide rather than serve under a king — a Stoic self-killing
iron chains
capable of imprisoning; able to hold back
slave; bondsman
tyrannical predator — Cassius's stock image for Caesar
does; female deer — and by pun, servants/rustics
refuse; scraps; animal waste
prepared, steeled (in mind, not just in arms)
grinning informer; mocking tattler
a partisan working to remedy — to join a faction against wrongs
honourable but dangerous outcome — the assassination
the colonnade beside Pompey's Theatre — the historical site of Caesar's assassination (not the Capitol, as Shakespeare stages it)
joined; made one body (with us)
the magistrate's seat of the urban praetor — Brutus's office
the statue of Lucius Junius Brutus, expeller of the last king; Marcus Brutus's legendary ancestor, displayed prominently in the Forum
Decimus Junius Brutus Albinus, conspirator — a different Brutus from Marcus
Gaius Trebonius, conspirator; in 3.1 he detains Antony outside while the murder is done
Rome's first permanent stone theatre, built 55 BCE; adjoining the colonnade where the Senate sometimes met — where Caesar was killed
the finest transformation of base into gold — Brutus's name makes base deeds seem noble
conceived; understood

annotations.title: Roman prodigies and the theatre of omen
annotations.body:
Casca's catalogue of portents — the slave with the burning unscorched hand, the lion at the Capitol, men walking the streets in fire, the owl shrieking at noon — is a curated list. Plutarch's *Life of Caesar* §63 reports several of these as genuine Roman omens surrounding the assassination. For the Roman religious imagination, unnatural events were not random: the gods communicated through disturbances of the natural order, and priest-augurs interpreted the signs.

Shakespeare's choice to stage them through Casca — not a priest or augur — is significant. Casca is a skeptic in 1.2 ('it was Greek to me'), and here he is shaken into reading the storm religiously. The play's opening three scenes move the audience from plebeian comedy (1.1) to private temptation (1.2) to public cosmic disturbance (1.3) — each louder than the last. By the time we reach 2.1 in Brutus's garden, the decision to kill has been contextualised by an atmosphere in which the universe itself seems to be announcing that something is wrong.
sources: Mary Floyd-Wilson, *English Ethnicity and Race in Early Modern Drama* (2003)

annotations.title: Cassius's sky-defying and the Republican suicide
annotations.body:
Cassius bares his chest to the lightning and declares he will wear his dagger rather than bow to a king. This is the play's first articulation of the Roman Stoic case for suicide — the view that a free man's life must end on his own terms rather than a tyrant's. Cato the Younger, Portia's father, had killed himself at Utica in 46 BCE rather than accept Caesar's pardon; Cassius invokes the same logic.

The play tracks Brutus's slow arrival at this view across five acts. In 5.1 Brutus tells Cassius he would once have blamed Cato's suicide as cowardly; by 5.5 he falls on Strato's sword himself. The philosophical arc is the play's most careful study. Cassius announces the destination here; Brutus arrives four acts later.

The Stoic-suicide tradition has an afterlife. Joseph Addison's *Cato* (1713) — written partly against Caesarism — was the most-performed serious drama in eighteenth-century America; George Washington staged it for his troops at Valley Forge. The Roman case for self-killing as a political statement was republican shorthand for more than a millennium. *Julius Caesar* is where Shakespeare rehearses it.
sources: Miriam Griffin, 'Philosophy, Cato, and Roman Suicide', *G&R* 33 (1986)

annotations.title: The conspiracy's anatomy: how Shakespeare assembles it
annotations.body:
The recruitment pattern is efficient: Cassius has already moved 'Some certain of the noblest-minded Romans,' takes Casca as a new sworn member in the storm, meets Cinna to plant the forged letters for Brutus, and confirms the meeting at Pompey's porch. Shakespeare telescopes what was in Plutarch a many-month negotiation into one night's work.

The choice of Pompey's porch (historically, the colonnade attached to Pompey's Theatre) is carefully preserved — that is where Caesar was actually killed in 44 BCE, not at the Capitol where Shakespeare stages the murder in 3.1. Shakespeare moves the murder's setting for dramatic reasons (the Capitol is grander, more political) while keeping the conspirators' muster at the historically-correct site.

Casca's speech on alchemy (L160ff) — that Brutus's involvement will 'change to virtue' any appearance of offence — is the conspiracy's necessary condition. The conspirators need Brutus for legitimacy, not ability; his moral capital will transmute the murder from assassination to civic act. Whether the transmutation actually happens is the question 3.2 will settle.

trials.prompt: Which of these is NOT one of Casca's reported prodigies?
options: A slave's left hand burning without being scorched
A lion glaring at Casca near the Capitol
A ghost of Caesar appearing in the Forum
An owl shrieking at noon in the market-place

trials.prompt: Cassius's baring his breast to the lightning and threatening 'I know where I will wear this dagger' is:
options: A theatrical gesture without philosophical content
The play's first explicit articulation of the Roman Stoic case for suicide as resistance to tyranny
A threat against Casca
Invocation of a storm-god

trials.prompt: Why does Cassius need Brutus in the conspiracy so much more than he needs any other individual man?
options: Brutus is the best swordsman
Brutus's reputation — 'like richest alchemy' — will transmute the assassination into a civic act; he supplies legitimacy the other conspirators cannot
Brutus controls Rome's finances
Brutus has the key to the Capitol

trials.prompt: Where does Cassius tell Cinna the conspirators are meeting?
options: The Senate house
Pompey's porch — the colonnade beside Pompey's Theatre, and historically the actual site of Caesar's assassination (though Shakespeare later stages the murder at the Capitol)
Brutus's garden
The Capitol

trials.prompt: What rhetorical effect does Shakespeare achieve by placing the prodigies scene between Cassius's private seduction of Brutus and the garden soliloquy of 2.1?
options: It fills time
It escalates the setting from private conversation to cosmic disturbance, so that the decision in 2.1 arrives under an atmosphere of universe-wide upheaval
It introduces comic relief
It resolves the main plot early
